title: Message Queues
summary: Kafka vs RabbitMQ, pub/sub, ordering guarantees, idempotency, at-least-once vs exactly-once delivery.

sections.content:
## Why Message Queues?

Message queues decouple producers from consumers, enabling:
- **Async processing** — return fast, process slowly
- **Load leveling** — smooth out traffic spikes
- **Fault tolerance** — messages persist even if consumer is down
- **Fan-out** — one event → many consumers

sections.title: Message Queue — Decoupling Producers and Consumers

rows.Apache Kafka: Event streaming, audit log, high throughput

sections.title: Kafka Architecture
sections.content:
graph LR
    Prod[Producer] -->|write| T[Topic: orders\nPartition 0\nPartition 1\nPartition 2]
    T -->|Consumer Group A\nanalytics| CGA1[Consumer 1]
    T -->|Consumer Group A| CGA2[Consumer 2]
    T -->|Consumer Group B\nemail| CGB[Consumer 3]
    Zk[ZooKeeper / KRaft\nBroker coordination]
    style T fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff

## Kafka Ordering

Kafka guarantees ordering **within a partition**, not across partitions.

```java
// All events for same order → same partition → guaranteed order
ProducerRecord<String, OrderEvent> record = new ProducerRecord<>(
    "orders",
    order.getId().toString(), // partition key — same order ID → same partition
    event
);
```

## Consumer Groups

```
Topic: orders (3 partitions)
Consumer Group: fulfillment

Partition 0 → Consumer A  (1:1 assignment)
Partition 1 → Consumer B
Partition 2 → Consumer C

Scale: add Consumer D → Kafka rebalances partitions
De-scale: remove Consumer C → its partition reassigned to remaining consumers
```

sections.title: Use Kafka for event streaming, RabbitMQ for task queues
sections.content: If you need to replay events, build event-sourced systems, or handle millions of messages per second — use Kafka. If you need complex routing, priority queues, RPC patterns, or per-message acknowledgment with short retention — use RabbitMQ.

sections.question: How do you achieve effectively exactly-once semantics with an at-least-once message queue?
options: Use transactions in the message broker
Make consumers idempotent — processing the same message twice produces the same result
Use a separate exactly-once queue for critical messages
sections.explanation: The practical approach to exactly-once is: use at-least-once delivery (simpler, more available) AND make consumers idempotent. If a message is processed twice, the second processing has no effect (e.g. by tracking processed event IDs). This gives you exactly-once business semantics without the complexity of distributed exactly-once protocols.
